raw_response:
Looking at this causal inference problem, I need to identify confounders - variables that affect both insurance type (Medicaid vs. private) and in-hospital mortality, while avoiding mediators and colliders.

For insurance type selection, key factors include:
- Demographics (age, race, marital status) - these affect eligibility and choice
- Baseline health status/comorbidities - sicker patients may be more likely to have Medicaid
- Time period - policy changes over time

These same factors also directly affect mortality risk, making them confounders that need adjustment.

I should exclude:
- Language (more likely a collider - affected by race/ethnicity which also affects insurance)
- Sex (less clear relationship to insurance type selection in adults)
- Some very rare conditions that may not be strong confounders

ADJUSTMENT SET:

age_at_admission, race_white, race_black, race_hispanic, race_asian, race_other, marital_status_married, period_2011_2013, period_2014_2016, period_2017_2019, period_2020_2022, chf, arrhythmia, valvular, pulm_circ, pvd, hypertension, paralysis, neuro_other, copd, diabetes_uncomplicated, diabetes_complicated, hypo_thyroid, renal_failure, liver_disease, lymphoma, metastatic_cancer, solid_tumor, coagulopathy, obesity, weight_loss, fluid_electrolyte, blood_loss_anemia, deficiency_anemia, alcohol_abuse, drug_abuse, psychosis, depression
